content.content:
### task_setting: Input: a background context B (problems, motivations, experimental settings, constraints) plus an optional seed term v that should be a focus of the idea. Output: a full natural-language sentence describing a novel scientific idea/hypothesis/research direction that is grounded in literature and novel with respect to B and the broader corpus. This is a departure from prior literature-based discovery, which only did binary link prediction between concept pairs (e.g., drug-disease links). Primary domain is AI/NLP; a case study generalizes to the biomedical/biochemical domain.
### methodology: SCIMON first builds training data via scientific information extraction: it classifies abstract sentences into Background/Method/Objective (using Cohan et al. 2019), treats Background as input and the rest as target ideas, and extracts Task/Method/Material/Metric entities and used-for relations as seed/target terms. At inference, an Inspiration Retrieval module retrieves three kinds of inspirations: (1) Semantic Neighbors (target terms of semantically similar past problems via SentenceBERT all-mpnet-base-v2), (2) KG Neighbors (adjacent task/method nodes from a global background knowledge graph of used-for relations), and (3) Citation Neighbors (titles of cited papers). A Generation module (in-context GPT-3.5/GPT-4 zero/few-shot/retrieval, or fine-tuned T5 with a new in-context contrastive InfoNCE objective that penalizes copying from the background) produces ideas. Finally, an Iterative Novelty Boosting step retrieves the k=20 nearest existing literature ideas, and if any exceed a cosine-similarity threshold (theta=0.6) it instructs the model to make the idea significantly different and re-generates, iterating until all retrieved similarities fall below threshold. Temporal train/dev/test split (<2021 / 2021 / 2022) avoids contamination.
### datasets: ['NLP corpus: 67,408 ACL Anthology papers from S2ORC (Lo et al. 2020)', 'Dataset statistics: Train 114,310 instances (55,884 forward + 58,426 backward), Valid 16,195, Test 5,309', 'Gold test subset: 194 manually curated challenging instances (low background-target similarity, <=0.074 ~10th percentile)', 'Biomedical/biochemical corpus from PubMed (1988 to 2024/01), IE via PubTator 3; test split after 2023/08 (Meditron-7b cutoff)']
### baselines: ['GPT-3.5 (davinci-003) zero-shot (GPT3.5ZS)', 'GPT-3.5 few-shot random (GPT3.5FS)', 'GPT-3.5 few-shot semantic retrieval (GPT3.5Retr)', 'GPT-4 (gpt-4-0314) zero-shot (GPT4ZS)', 'GPT-4 few-shot (GPT4FS)', 'Fine-tuned T5 (Raffel et al. 2020)', 'T5+SN+CL (semantic-neighbor + contrastive) and other T5 variants', 'GPT4 variants with inspirations: +SN (semantic neighbors), +KG (KG neighbors), +CT (citation)', 'Meditron-7b (Chen et al. 2023) fine-tuned, for biomedical domain']
### metrics: ['Human evaluation: helpful/unhelpful binary rating (relevance, novelty, clarity, reasonableness) - Study I', 'Human pairwise ranking of technical detail and novelty vs. each other and vs. ground-truth papers - Study II', 'Human evaluation of iterative novelty boosting: % ideas made different, % made more novel, # new terms added - Study III', 'Automated metrics: ROUGE, BERTScore, BARTScore (similarity to ground truth)', 'Biomedical human eval: helpful %, vs. ground truth %']
### key_results: Study I: GPT4FS and GPT4FS+KG outperform other models by a wide margin (GPT4FS helpful 73%/unhelpful 27% region; GPT4FS+KG 66%/34%); among non-GPT models T5+SN+CL performs best; GPT-3.5 variants worst (e.g., 3FS 33% helpful). A preliminary check found GPT4-FS ranked higher than GPT4-ZS in 65% of cases, so zero-shot GPT-4 was dropped. Study II: GPT4FS+KG had higher technical detail in 48% of pairs and was less incremental (more novel) in 45%; most remaining were ties. Critically, in 85% of comparisons the original ground-truth paper idea was rated significantly higher in technical level and novelty than the generated idea. Study III (iterative novelty boosting): for SN, 88.9% of 1st-iteration ideas were substantially different and 54.4%/55.6% gained novelty (per Table 3, GPT4FS +54.4%, +SN +55.6%, +CT +47.8%, +KG +46.7%); 2nd iteration on SN gained novelty for 57.8% more; ~22-23 new terms added per idea after filtering; ideas never dropped in novelty (either increased or maintained). Biomedical case study: ~80% of generated directions rated positively; evaluators sometimes preferred generated outputs over ground truth on technical detail (vs-GT: +SN 45%, +CT 50%). Automated metrics: T5-based models outperform GPT-based on ROUGE/BERTScore/BARTScore (GPT-4 outputs longer, score lower automatically but win human eval); the in-context contrastive objective reduced copying and improved over baseline fine-tuning.
### code_url: https://github.com/eaglew/clbd
### relevance_to_hypothesis: Strongly supportive and central to the hypothesis. SCIMON's core thesis is that abstract/ungrounded prompting of LLMs (e.g., zero-shot GPT-4) produces low-novelty, low-depth ideas, and that GROUNDING novelty improves idea quality. It grounds novelty two ways: (1) literature-comparison / outcome-style refinement - the iterative retrieve-compare-update loop explicitly compares each generated idea to prior papers and forces it to be 'significantly different,' which human eval shows raises novelty for 46-58% of ideas without ever lowering it; and (2) retrieval-of-inspirations from semantic-neighbor, knowledge-graph, and citation sources, which grounds generation in existing concepts (GPT4FS+KG and T5+SN+CL are the strongest variants, beating ungrounded baselines). Thus it provides concrete evidence that operationalizing/grounding the notion of novelty via literature comparison and inspiration retrieval yields better, more novel ideas than abstract prompting. It also tempers the hypothesis by showing grounding alone does not close the large gap to human-authored ideas (still 85% in favor of ground truth).

tool_use_result.stdout:
### task_setting: Input: a background context B (problems, motivations, experimental settings, constraints) plus an optional seed term v that should be a focus of the idea. Output: a full natural-language sentence describing a novel scientific idea/hypothesis/research direction that is grounded in literature and novel with respect to B and the broader corpus. This is a departure from prior literature-based discovery, which only did binary link prediction between concept pairs (e.g., drug-disease links). Primary domain is AI/NLP; a case study generalizes to the biomedical/biochemical domain.
### methodology: SCIMON first builds training data via scientific information extraction: it classifies abstract sentences into Background/Method/Objective (using Cohan et al. 2019), treats Background as input and the rest as target ideas, and extracts Task/Method/Material/Metric entities and used-for relations as seed/target terms. At inference, an Inspiration Retrieval module retrieves three kinds of inspirations: (1) Semantic Neighbors (target terms of semantically similar past problems via SentenceBERT all-mpnet-base-v2), (2) KG Neighbors (adjacent task/method nodes from a global background knowledge graph of used-for relations), and (3) Citation Neighbors (titles of cited papers). A Generation module (in-context GPT-3.5/GPT-4 zero/few-shot/retrieval, or fine-tuned T5 with a new in-context contrastive InfoNCE objective that penalizes copying from the background) produces ideas. Finally, an Iterative Novelty Boosting step retrieves the k=20 nearest existing literature ideas, and if any exceed a cosine-similarity threshold (theta=0.6) it instructs the model to make the idea significantly different and re-generates, iterating until all retrieved similarities fall below threshold. Temporal train/dev/test split (<2021 / 2021 / 2022) avoids contamination.
### datasets: ['NLP corpus: 67,408 ACL Anthology papers from S2ORC (Lo et al. 2020)', 'Dataset statistics: Train 114,310 instances (55,884 forward + 58,426 backward), Valid 16,195, Test 5,309', 'Gold test subset: 194 manually curated challenging instances (low background-target similarity, <=0.074 ~10th percentile)', 'Biomedical/biochemical corpus from PubMed (1988 to 2024/01), IE via PubTator 3; test split after 2023/08 (Meditron-7b cutoff)']
### baselines: ['GPT-3.5 (davinci-003) zero-shot (GPT3.5ZS)', 'GPT-3.5 few-shot random (GPT3.5FS)', 'GPT-3.5 few-shot semantic retrieval (GPT3.5Retr)', 'GPT-4 (gpt-4-0314) zero-shot (GPT4ZS)', 'GPT-4 few-shot (GPT4FS)', 'Fine-tuned T5 (Raffel et al. 2020)', 'T5+SN+CL (semantic-neighbor + contrastive) and other T5 variants', 'GPT4 variants with inspirations: +SN (semantic neighbors), +KG (KG neighbors), +CT (citation)', 'Meditron-7b (Chen et al. 2023) fine-tuned, for biomedical domain']
### metrics: ['Human evaluation: helpful/unhelpful binary rating (relevance, novelty, clarity, reasonableness) - Study I', 'Human pairwise ranking of technical detail and novelty vs. each other and vs. ground-truth papers - Study II', 'Human evaluation of iterative novelty boosting: % ideas made different, % made more novel, # new terms added - Study III', 'Automated metrics: ROUGE, BERTScore, BARTScore (similarity to ground truth)', 'Biomedical human eval: helpful %, vs. ground truth %']
### key_results: Study I: GPT4FS and GPT4FS+KG outperform other models by a wide margin (GPT4FS helpful 73%/unhelpful 27% region; GPT4FS+KG 66%/34%); among non-GPT models T5+SN+CL performs best; GPT-3.5 variants worst (e.g., 3FS 33% helpful). A preliminary check found GPT4-FS ranked higher than GPT4-ZS in 65% of cases, so zero-shot GPT-4 was dropped. Study II: GPT4FS+KG had higher technical detail in 48% of pairs and was less incremental (more novel) in 45%; most remaining were ties. Critically, in 85% of comparisons the original ground-truth paper idea was rated significantly higher in technical level and novelty than the generated idea. Study III (iterative novelty boosting): for SN, 88.9% of 1st-iteration ideas were substantially different and 54.4%/55.6% gained novelty (per Table 3, GPT4FS +54.4%, +SN +55.6%, +CT +47.8%, +KG +46.7%); 2nd iteration on SN gained novelty for 57.8% more; ~22-23 new terms added per idea after filtering; ideas never dropped in novelty (either increased or maintained). Biomedical case study: ~80% of generated directions rated positively; evaluators sometimes preferred generated outputs over ground truth on technical detail (vs-GT: +SN 45%, +CT 50%). Automated metrics: T5-based models outperform GPT-based on ROUGE/BERTScore/BARTScore (GPT-4 outputs longer, score lower automatically but win human eval); the in-context contrastive objective reduced copying and improved over baseline fine-tuning.
### code_url: https://github.com/eaglew/clbd
### relevance_to_hypothesis: Strongly supportive and central to the hypothesis. SCIMON's core thesis is that abstract/ungrounded prompting of LLMs (e.g., zero-shot GPT-4) produces low-novelty, low-depth ideas, and that GROUNDING novelty improves idea quality. It grounds novelty two ways: (1) literature-comparison / outcome-style refinement - the iterative retrieve-compare-update loop explicitly compares each generated idea to prior papers and forces it to be 'significantly different,' which human eval shows raises novelty for 46-58% of ideas without ever lowering it; and (2) retrieval-of-inspirations from semantic-neighbor, knowledge-graph, and citation sources, which grounds generation in existing concepts (GPT4FS+KG and T5+SN+CL are the strongest variants, beating ungrounded baselines). Thus it provides concrete evidence that operationalizing/grounding the notion of novelty via literature comparison and inspiration retrieval yields better, more novel ideas than abstract prompting. It also tempers the hypothesis by showing grounding alone does not close the large gap to human-authored ideas (still 85% in favor of ground truth).

content.content:
Literature Meets Data: A Synergistic Approach to Hypothesis Generation
Haokun Liu♣∗, Yangqiaoyu Zhou♣∗, Mingxuan Li♣∗, Chenfei Yuan†
& Chenhao Tan♣
Department of Computer Science
University of Chicago♣, Tsinghua University†
Chicago, IL 60637, USA
{haokunliu, zhouy1, mingxuanl, chenhao}@uchicago.edu,
yuancf21@mails.tsinghua.edu.cn
Abstract
AI holds promise for transforming scientific
processes, including hypothesis generation.
Prior work on hypothesis generation can be
broadly categorized into theory-driven and data-
driven approaches. While both have proven
effective in generating novel and plausible hy-
potheses, it remains an open question whether
they can complement each other. To address
this, we develop the first method that combines
literature-based insights with data to perform
LLM-powered hypothesis generation. We ap-
ply our method on five different datasets and
demonstrate that integrating literature and data
outperforms other baselines (8.97% over few-
shot, 15.75% over literature-based alone, and
3.37% over data-driven alone). Additionally,
we conduct the first human evaluation to as-
sess the utility of LLM-generated hypotheses
in assisting human decision-making on two
challenging tasks: deception detection and AI
generated content detection. Our results show
that human accuracy improves significantly
by 7.44% and 14.19% on these tasks, respec-
tively. These findings suggest that integrat-
ing literature-based and data-driven approaches
provides a comprehensive and nuanced frame-
work for hypothesis generation and could open
new avenues for scientific inquiry.
1 Introduction
“It is the theory that decides what can be
observed.” —Albert Einstein
Large language models (LLMs) excel at syn-
thesizing information and hold promise for trans-
forming hypothesis generation, a critical yet un-
derstudied step in scientific discoveries. Many re-
cent studies recognize this potential and use LLMs
to generate hypotheses (e.g., Yang et al., 2024b;
Batista and Ross, 2024). We broadly categorize
them into theory-driven and data-driven methods.
*Equal contributions.
On one hand, theory-driven approaches leverage
LLMs to review existing literature and generate
novel hypotheses/ideas (Yang et al., 2024b; Baek
et al., 2024). These methods have shown promising
results in terms of the hypotheses’ novelty, valid-
ity, and usefulness to researchers, while remain-
ing grounded in established human knowledge (Si
et al., 2024). However, they come with notable lim-
itations: they require high-quality literature, strug-
gle to adapt to new data, and lack empirical support.
Data-driven approaches, on the other hand, propose
hypotheses by discovering patterns in data (Zhou
et al., 2024; Qiu et al., 2024). These hypotheses are
data-adaptive and can exhibit strong performance
in explaining the data. However, they could be too
overly tailored to the specific datasets used, which
can hinder their generalizability.
We hypothesize that theory can guide the discov-
ery from data and propose to integrate literature-
based and data-driven hypothesis generation (see
Figure 1). For the data-driven component, we use
HYPO GENI C as the backbone (Zhou et al., 2024).
HYPO GENI C leverages an LLM to initialize hy-
potheses from a small number of examples and
then updates them iteratively to improve the qual-
ity of hypotheses. To enhance this process with
literature insights, we introduce a literature-based
hypothesis agent. This agent interacts with the
data-driven hypothesis agent (HYPO GENI C), refin-
ing and maintaining a shared pool of hypotheses
through continuous collaboration, ensuring that the
hypotheses benefit from both data-driven adaptabil-
ity and the grounding of existing scientific knowl-
edge. In addition to the refinement approach, we
also propose to directly unionize literature-based
and data-driven hypotheses.
To comprehensively evaluate these hypotheses,
we conduct automatic and human evaluation to as-
sess their generalizability, utility, and novelty. We
apply our method to address research questions in
social sciences: deception detection, AI generated
arXiv:2410.17309v3  [cs.AI]  8 Jan 2025
Figure 1: The literature-based approach (A) leverages existing human knowledge to generate hypotheses but
struggles to adapt to new data and lacks empirical grounding. The data-driven approach (B) relies on large datasets
to generate hypotheses, enabling adaptation to diverse scenarios but risking overfitting. The literature + data
approach (C) combines the strengths of both, grounding hypotheses in human knowledge while incorporating
empirical data to enhance adaptiveness. See algorithmic details in § 2.
content (AIGC) detection, mental stress detection,
and persuasive argument prediction. Automatic
evaluation results show that integrating literature
and data outperforms other baselines: 8.97% over
few-shot, 15.75% over literature-based alone, and
3.37% over data-driven alone in accuracy on out-of-
distribution datasets, a measure of generalizability.
Moreover, we conduct the first study to assess
the utility of AI-generated hypotheses in improving
human decision-making and show that our gener-
ated hypotheses improve human accuracy by 7.44%
and 14.19% on deception detection and AIGC de-
tection. Additionally, we find that literature-based
and data-driven hypotheses complement each other,
as one set often contains novel information not
found in the other set.
In sum, we make the following contributions:
• We propose the first approach to using both lit-
erature information and data for LLM-powered
hypothesis generation.
• We conduct automatic evaluation to assess the
utility of the generated hypotheses in improving
LLM predictions. Experiments on five datasets
demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach.
• We conduct the first human evaluation to test the
utility of LLM-generated hypotheses and demon-
strate consistent improvements on two challeng-
ing tasks.
The code and data are available at
https://github.com/ChicagoHAI/
hypothesis-generation.
2 Methods
We formulate the problem of hypothesis generation
as follows. Assuming that we have access to liter-
ature L and observational data D that are relevant
to a research question q. Then, we want to develop
an AI-powered algorithm f with model M such
that we can generate high-quality hypotheses for
the research question q, i.e., H = fM(q, L, D).
Example research questions include what makes an
argument persuasive and what signs are indicative
of AI-

PAGES 12
Evaluating and Enhancing Large Language Models for Novelty Assessment
in Scholarly Publications
Ethan Lin *
Santa Clara University
Santa Clara, CA, USA
enlin@scu.edu
Zhiyuan Peng *
Santa Clara University
Santa Clara, CA, USA
zpeng@scu.edu
Yi Fang
Santa Clara University
Santa Clara, CA, USA
yfang@scu.edu
Abstract
Recent studies have evaluated the creativ-
ity/novelty of large language models (LLMs)
primarily from a semantic perspective, using
benchmarks from cognitive science. However,
accessing the novelty in scholarly publications
is a largely unexplored area in evaluating LLMs.
In this paper, we introduce a scholarly novelty
benchmark (SchNovel 1) to evaluate LLMs’
ability to assess novelty in scholarly papers.
SchNovel consists of 15000 pairs of papers
across six fields sampled from the arXiv dataset
with publication dates spanning 2 to 10 years
apart. In each pair, the more recently published
paper is assumed to be more novel. Addition-
ally, we propose RAG-Novelty, which simu-
lates the review process taken by human re-
viewers by leveraging the retrieval of similar
papers to assess novelty. Extensive experiments
provide insights into the capabilities of differ-
ent LLMs to assess novelty and demonstrate
that RAG-Novelty outperforms recent baseline
models.
1 Introduction
Creativity, traditionally defined as the capability of
producing innovative ideas, forming unique con-
nections, and developing solutions that are both
novel and effective (Runco and Jaeger, 2012), was
thought to be exclusive to humans. With the advent
of large language models (LLMs) (OpenAI, 2023;
Dubey et al., 2024b), however, this assumption
has been challenged as LLMs have demonstrated
remarkable proficiency in following human instruc-
tions to address a wide range of tasks, such as
open-domain question answering, coding, solving
math problems, and even utilizing external tools
like search engines when necessary (Asai et al.,
2024). In a recent study, Si et al. (2024) showed
that LLMs can generate research ideas that are
*These authors contributed equally to this work.
1The SchNovel dataset and RAG-Novelty code are avail-
able at: https://github.com/ethannlin/SchNovel.
more novel than those produced by human experts.
While their generative capabilities are impressive,
they also raise concerns. For instance, some artists
are uncomfortable with LLMs producing artwork
that closely mimics established styles, prompting
debates about whether LLMs can genuinely be con-
sidered creative.
Recent studies evaluating the generative creativ-
ity of LLMs have yielded inconsistent conclusions.
Orwig et al. (2024) concluded that GPT-4 (Ope-
nAI, 2023) generates stories that are comparable
to those written by humans in terms of creativity.
Similarly, Pépin et al. (2024) found that LLMs can
even surpass humans in specific creative tasks, such
as divergent association and creative writing. How-
ever, Anderson et al. (2024) argued that AI-based
creativity support tools (CSTs) like ChatGPT are
not yet well-suited to fostering truly original ideas,
as they can lead to homogenization of human cre-
ativity. Chakrabarty et al. (2024) observed that
LLM-generated stories pass the Torrance Test

They also propose simpler UNION methods that directly unionize literature-only hypotheses with data-driven ones (Literature∪HypoGeniC, Literature∪HypoRefine). import json
notes=json.load(open("/tmp/notes_map.json"))
cur={c['label']:c for c in json.load(open("/tmp/curated_full.json"))}
order=["can_llms_generate_novel_ideas_si2024","scimon_novelty_wang2023","hypothesis_generation_zhou_tan2024",
"open_domain_hypotheses_yang2023","literature_meets_data2024","hypobench2025","literature_grounded_novelty_assessment2025",
"eval_enhance_novelty_assessment2024","moose_chem_yang2024","sga_bilevel_optimizers2024",
"researchagent_baek2024","chain_of_ideas2024","nova_iterative_planning2024","scideator_facet_recombination",
"hyper_literature_grounded2025","zero_shot_hypothesis_proposers2023","learning_generate_idea_dynamic_control2024",
"ideabench2024","researchbench2025","survey_hypothesis_generation_llm2025"]
def fl(x):
    if not x: return "—"
    if isinstance(x,str): return x
    return "; ".join(str(i) for i in x)
out=[]
for i,lab in enumerate(order,1):
    n=notes.get(lab); c=cur.get(lab,{})
    out.append(f"#### {i}. {n.get('title')}")
    out.append(f"- **arXiv / Year**: [{c.get('arxiv','')}](https://arxiv.org/abs/{c.get('arxiv','')}) / {n.get('year','')}  ·  citations≈{c.get('citations','?')}  ·  relevance {c.get('relevance','?')}/3")
    out.append(f"- **Authors**: {n.get('authors','')}")
    out.append(f"- **Grounding mechanism(s)**: {fl(n.get('grounding_mechanisms'))}")
    out.append(f"- **Task / setting**: {n.get('task_setting','')}")
    out.append(f"- **Method**: {n.get('methodology','')}")
    out.append(f"- **Datasets**: {fl(n.get('datasets'))}")
    out.append(f"- **Baselines**: {fl(n.get('baselines'))}")
    out.append(f"- **Metrics**: {fl(n.get('metrics'))}")
    out.append(f"- **Key results**: {n.get('key_results','')}")
    out.append(f"- **Limitations**: {n.get('limitations','')}")
    out.append(f"- **Code**: {n.get('code_url') or '—'}")
    out.append(f"- **Relevance to hypothesis**: {n.get('relevance_to_hypothesis','')}")
    if n.get("_corrected"): out.append(f"- *(Note: {n['_corrected']})*")
    out.append("")
open("/tmp/perpaper.md","w").write("\n".join(out))
print("per-paper section ready:", len(out), "lines")
EOF

input.content:
# Literature Review — Scientific Idea Generation with Grounded Novelty

**Hypothesis under investigation.** *Large language models generate better scientific ideas when the
notion of novelty is **grounded** — through deduction, induction, proposal writing, outcome imagination,
small experiments, or literature comparison — rather than relying on abstract or ungrounded prompts.*

This review synthesizes 20 papers (curated from 218 unique candidates returned by paper-finder + arXiv
search) that bear directly on (a) *generating* ideas/hypotheses with LLMs, (b) *grounding* that
generation in literature, data, or experiments, and (c) *evaluating* novelty. Per-paper structured notes
(method, datasets, baselines, metrics, results) are in the second half of this document; the raw catalog
is in `resources.md` and `papers/README.md`.

---

## 1. Research Area Overview

LLM-based scientific ideation has moved, in roughly two years (2023→2025), from "can an LLM emit a
plausible hypothesis from a prompt?" to "how do we *ground* and *evaluate* ideas so that novelty is real
and useful, not just surface-level?" Three threads have emerged, and the surveys in this set
(arXiv:2504.05496; the Evolving-Roles survey) organize the field the same way:

- **Theory-driven / literature-grounded generation.** Retrieve prior work and generate ideas grounded in
  it, explicitly optimizing or checking novelty against the corpus. Canonical: **SciMON** (iterative
  retrieve–compare–revise until "sufficiently novel"), **ResearchAgent**, **Chain-of-Ideas**, **Nova**,
  **MOOSE / MOOSE-Chem** (decompose `hypothesis = background + inspirations`), **HypER**, **Scideator**
  (facet recombination). Grounding modes: *literature-comparison*, *retrieval*, *deduction*.
- **Data-driven / inductive generation.** Induce explanatory hypotheses from labeled examples and judge
  them by *downstream predictive accuracy* — a grounded signal of usefulness. Canonical: **HypoGeniC**
  (Zhou & Tan), **open-domain MOOSE** (Yang et al.), **HypoBench**. Grounding modes: *induction*,
  *small-experiments / outcome-checking*.
- **Synergistic & agentic.** Combine the two: **Literature Meets Data** (HypoRefine + Union) shows
  literature and data are *complementary*; **SGA** closes the loop with a simulator
  (*outcome-imagination + small-experiments*); **Learning-to-Generate-Ideas** adds explicit
  novelty/feasibility control.

In parallel, an **evaluation** literature has grown because "is this novel?" prompts are unreliable: the
landmark human study **Si et al. 2024** (100+ NLP researchers), the **SchNovel / RAG-Novelty** benchmark,
**Literature-Grounded Novelty Assessment**, and idea/hypothesis benchmarks **IdeaBench**, **ResearchBench**,
**HypoBench**. The consistent message: *grounding the novelty judgment in retrieved related work beats
abstract LLM-as-judge prompting* — the evaluation-side analogue of the project hypothesis.

## 2. How the six grounding mechanisms appear in the literature

The hypothesis names six grounding strategies. Mapping them onto concrete, reusable instantiations:

| Grounding mechanism | What it means operationally | Best exemplars in this set |
|---|---|---|
| **Literature comparison** | Retrieve prior work; force the idea to differ from it / score novelty against it | SciMON (novelty-boosting loop), Chain-of-Ideas, Nova, RAG-Novelty, Literature-Grounded Novelty Assessment |
| **Induction** | Generate hypotheses *from data/examples*, refine by evidence | HypoGeniC, open-domain MOOSE, HypoBench, Literature Meets Data |
| **Proposal writing** | Produce a full structured proposal (problem→method→experiments), critiqued and revised | ResearchAgent, Si et al. agent, Scideator |
| **Outcome imagination** | Predict/imagine experimental outcomes before committing | SGA (simulation feedback), Chain-of-Ideas (anticipated trends), HypoGeniC reward (predicted accuracy) |
| **Small experiments** | Run a cheap empirical check and feed results back | HypoGeniC / Literature Meets Data (downstream-accuracy as the check), SGA (differentiable simulation), HypER |
| **Deduction / decomposition** | Decompose the discovery into sub-steps and reason structurally | MOOSE-Chem (`background + inspirations`), HypoRefine |

**Ungrounded baseline** is well-represented too: *zero-shot / few-shot "propose a novel idea" prompting*
(LLMs-are-Zero-Shot-Hypothesis-Proposers; the zero-shot arms of SciMON, MOOSE, HypoGeniC). Every grounded
method in this set is measured *against* such a prompt — so the hypothesis is directly testable with
existing baselines.

## 3. What the evidence says about the hypothesis (the through-line)

The papers provide repeated, quantitative support that **grounding helps**, with one important caveat:

- **SciMON**: zero-shot GPT-4 ideas are low-novelty/low-depth; adding inspiration retrieval (KG/semantic)
  and an *iterative novelty-boosting loop* (compare to k=20 nearest prior ideas, force "significantly
  different") raises human-judged novelty for **46–58%** of ideas and **never lowers it**.
- **MOOSE (open-domain, Yang et al.)**: novelty rises from **2.48 → 3.39** (expert-confirmed direction)
  as multi-step feedback grounding is added, *while validity stays ~3.9* — grounding raises novelty
  without sacrificing plausibility.
- **HypoGeniC (Zhou & Tan)**: inductive, data-grounded hypotheses beat few-shot prompting by
  **3.3–31.7%** accuracy and even beat fine-tuned RoBERTa/Llama-2 oracles trained on 1000 examples.
- **Literature Meets Data**: literature + data > few-shot by **8.97%**, > literature-alone by **15.75%**,
  > data-alone by **3.37%** (OOD); and grounded hypotheses improve *human* accuracy by up to **14.19%**.
- **HypoBench** (194 datasets, 12 domains): data-driven and literature+data methods dominate
  inference-only and zero-shot-generation baselines, especially OOD.
- **Evaluation side** — **RAG-Novelty** and **Literature-Grounded Novelty Assessment** (F1 0.79, κ 0.59,
  acc 0.81 vs. 0.68 zero-shot) both show grounding the *judgment* in retrieved papers beats ungrounded
  LLM-as-judge.

**The caveat (critical for experiment design).** *Novelty ≠ value.* Si et al. find AI ideas are judged
**more novel** (5.64 vs 4.84, p<0.01) but **slightly less feasible** than experts'; their follow-up
**Ideation–Execution Gap** shows that once ideas are actually executed, the LLM novelty advantage
*shrinks and rankings can flip*. SciMON shows grounding still leaves an **85%** gap to real paper ideas on
technical depth. **Implication:** the project should measure not just a novelty score but *grounded
usefulness* (downstream accuracy, ground-truth recovery, feasibility) — exactly what the "outcome
imagination / small experiments / literature comparison" grounding modes provide.

## 4. Common Methodologies

- **Retrieve-then-generate** (RAG over a paper corpus / KG): SciMON, ResearchAgent, Chain-of-Ideas, Nova,
  HypER, MOOSE-Chem.
- **Iterative refine-and-check loops**: compare to prior work and revise (SciMON novelty boosting), or
  update from data via a bandit reward (HypoGeniC), or agent–reviewer debate (ResearchAgent, Nova).
- **Decomposition**: `hypothesis = background + inspirations` (MOOSE-Chem); facet recombination
  (Scideator); literature∪data union (Literature Meets Data).
- **Closed-loop with experiments/simulation**: SGA bilevel optimization; downstream-accuracy as the
  empirical check (HypoGeniC/HypoBench/Literature Meets Data).
- **Controllable generation**: explicit novelty/feasibility dimensions (Learning-to-Generate-Ideas).

## 5. Standard Baselines (use these)

1. **Zero-shot prompting** — "propose a novel hypothesis for q." (ungrounded; the key control)
2. **Few-shot prompting** — a handful of demonstrations.
3. **Literature-only generation** — retrieve + generate, no data/experiment grounding.
4. **Data-only generation** — HypoGeniC (induction), no literature.
5. **Supervised oracle** (for data tasks) — fine-tuned RoBERTa / Llama as an upper-bound reference.
6. **Strong grounded systems** as toplines — SciMON, HypoRefine/Union, MOOSE-Chem.

## 6. Evaluation Metrics (use a mix; do not rely on a single novelty score)

- **Grounded utility**: downstream classification accuracy (IND + **OOD** for generalizability);
  human-decision-improvement; **Hypothesis/Feature Discovery Rate** and **Relationship Correctness**
  (HypoBench); rediscovery similarity to held-out ground-truth hypotheses (MOOSE-Chem).
- **Novelty / quality (human or LLM-judge)**: Novelty, Excitement, Feasibility, Expected-Effectiveness,
  Overall on 1–10 (Si et al.); Validness/Novelty/Helpfulness 1–5 (MOOSE); pairwise novelty (SchNovel).
- **Grounded novelty judging**: RAG-Novelty / Literature-Grounded assessment (retrieval-conditioned),
  reported with **Cohen's κ** for human agreement — preferred over bare LLM-as-judge.
- **Rigor**: blind review, multiple reviewers, Welch's t-test + Bonferroni, diversity/duplication rate
  (idea generators collapse to few unique ideas at scale — Si et al.).

## 7. Datasets in the Literature (and what we downloaded)

- **HypoGeniC / HypoBench tasks** (downloaded → `datasets/`): deceptive reviews, dreaddit (stress),
  AIGC detection (gptgc/llamagc), headline engagement, retweets, persuasive pairs, paper citations,
  journal; plus controllable **synthetic** tasks (election, admission, shoe, preference). HF-style JSON,
  shared across HypoGeniC, Literature Meets Data, and HypoBench → a single task vocabulary for ablations.
- **Hypothesis-agent literature** (downloaded): per-task PDFs + parsed papers for the literature-grounded
  conditions.
- **Idea-level benchmarks** (in cloned code / available): MOOSE-Chem (51 annotated 2024 chemistry papers),
  open-domain TOMATO (50 social-science papers), Si et al. ideation+review data, IdeaBench, ResearchBench,
  SchNovel (15k arXiv pairs).

## 8. Gaps and Opportunities (where this project can contribute)

1. **No head-to-head of grounding *mechanisms* under one protocol.** Each paper grounds *one* way and
   compares to ungrounded prompting; nobody has held task/model/metric fixed and ablated
   deduction vs. induction vs. proposal-writing vs. outcome-imagination vs. small-experiments vs.
   literature-comparison. **This is exactly the project's opening** — and the ChicagoHAI framework
   already implements the ungrounded baseline + induction + literature conditions on shared tasks.
2. **Novelty is over-trusted.** Given Si et al.'s ideation–execution flip, the project should report
   *grounded usefulness* alongside novelty, not novelty alone.
3. **Grounded *evaluation* of grounded *generation*.** Use RAG-Novelty / literature-grounded assessment
   to score ideas, avoiding the unreliable bare LLM-judge.
4. **Cost/benefit of grounding.** Outcome-imagination and small-experiments grounding cost compute;
   quantifying accuracy-per-token across mechanisms would be novel and practical.

## 9. Recommendations for Our Experiment

- **Backbone**: `code/hypogenic_chicagohai` (HypoGeniC + HypoRefine + Union). It already ships the
  *ungrounded baseline*, *induction*, and *literature-comparison* conditions on a shared task set with
  shared metrics — minimal glue to add the remaining grounding arms.
- **Datasets**: 3–4 HypoGeniC *real* tasks (deceptive_reviews, headline_binary, dreaddit/AIGC) + 2
  HypoBench *synthetic* controllable tasks (clean ground-truth recovery) + MOOSE-Chem for idea-level
  novelty. All already local in `datasets/` / `code/`.
- **Conditions (the ablation)**: (0) ungrounded zero/few-shot → (1) induction (HypoGeniC) →
  (2) literature-comparison (SciMON-style boosting / HypoRefine) → (3) literature+data Union →
  (4) outcome-imagination/small-experiment grounding (downstream-accuracy or SGA-style check) →
  (5) proposal-writing (full structured proposal + reviewer). Hold model and task fixed.
- **Metrics**: primary = downstream IND/OOD accuracy + HypoBench discovery rate / MOOSE-Chem rediscovery;
  secondary = grounded novelty (RAG-Novelty) + feasibility, with Cohen's κ for any human/LLM judging.
- **Models**: at least one strong API model + one open model (cross-model generalization, per HypoBench).
- **Statistics**: blind/randomized judging where human/LLM scores are used; Welch's t-test + multiple-
  comparison correction; report idea-diversity to catch mode collapse.

---

## 10. Per-Paper Notes

*(Structured notes extracted by a parallel deep-reading pass; the two ChicagoHAI/evaluation entries marked
"corrected" were re-read by the main agent after a reader confused* Literature Meets Data *with SciMON.)*

# Literature Review — Scientific Idea Generation with Grounded Novelty

**Hypothesis under investigation.** *Large language models generate better scientific ideas when the
notion of novelty is **grounded** — through deduction, induction, proposal writing, outcome imagination,
small experiments, or literature comparison — rather than relying on abstract or ungrounded prompts.*

This review synthesizes 20 papers (curated from 218 unique candidates returned by paper-finder + arXiv
search) that bear directly on (a) *generating* ideas/hypotheses with LLMs, (b) *grounding* that
generation in literature, data, or experiments, and (c) *evaluating* novelty. Per-paper structured notes
(method, datasets, baselines, metrics, results) are in the second half of this document; the raw catalog
is in `resources.md` and `papers/README.md`.

---

## 1. Research Area Overview

LLM-based scientific ideation has moved, in roughly two years (2023→2025), from "can an LLM emit a
plausible hypothesis from a prompt?" to "how do we *ground* and *evaluate* ideas so that novelty is real
and useful, not just surface-level?" Three threads have emerged, and the surveys in this set
(arXiv:2504.05496; the Evolving-Roles survey) organize the field the same way:

- **Theory-driven / literature-grounded generation.** Retrieve prior work and generate ideas grounded in
  it, explicitly optimizing or checking novelty against the corpus. Canonical: **SciMON** (iterative
  retrieve–compare–revise until "sufficiently novel"), **ResearchAgent**, **Chain-of-Ideas**, **Nova**,
  **MOOSE / MOOSE-Chem** (decompose `hypothesis = background + inspirations`), **HypER**, **Scideator**
  (facet recombination). Grounding modes: *literature-comparison*, *retrieval*, *deduction*.
- **Data-driven / inductive generation.** Induce explanatory hypotheses from labeled examples and judge
  them by *downstream predictive accuracy* — a grounded signal of usefulness. Canonical: **HypoGeniC**
  (Zhou & Tan), **open-domain MOOSE** (Yang et al.), **HypoBench**. Grounding modes: *induction*,
  *small-experiments / outcome-checking*.
- **Synergistic & agentic.** Combine the two: **Literature Meets Data** (HypoRefine + Union) shows
  literature and data are *complementary*; **SGA** closes the loop with a simulator
  (*outcome-imagination + small-experiments*); **Learning-to-Generate-Ideas** adds explicit
  novelty/feasibility control.

In parallel, an **evaluation** literature has grown because "is this novel?" prompts are unreliable: the
landmark human study **Si et al. 2024** (100+ NLP researchers), the **SchNovel / RAG-Novelty** benchmark,
**Literature-Grounded Novelty Assessment**, and idea/hypothesis benchmarks **IdeaBench**, **ResearchBench**,
**HypoBench**. The consistent message: *grounding the novelty judgment in retrieved related work beats
abstract LLM-as-judge prompting* — the evaluation-side analogue of the project hypothesis.

## 2. How the six grounding mechanisms appear in the literature

The hypothesis names six grounding strategies. Mapping them onto concrete, reusable instantiations:

| Grounding mechanism | What it means operationally | Best exemplars in this set |
|---|---|---|
| **Literature comparison** | Retrieve prior work; force the idea to differ from it / score novelty against it | SciMON (novelty-boosting loop), Chain-of-Ideas, Nova, RAG-Novelty, Literature-Grounded Novelty Assessment |
| **Induction** | Generate hypotheses *from data/examples*, refine by evidence | HypoGeniC, open-domain MOOSE, HypoBench, Literature Meets Data |
| **Proposal writing** | Produce a full structured proposal (problem→method→experiments), critiqued and revised | ResearchAgent, Si et al. agent, Scideator |
| **Outcome imagination** | Predict/imagine experimental outcomes before committing | SGA (simulation feedback), Chain-of-Ideas (anticipated trends), HypoGeniC reward (predicted accuracy) |
| **Small experiments** | Run a cheap empirical check and feed results back | HypoGeniC / Literature Meets Data (downstream-accuracy as the check), SGA (differentiable simulation), HypER |
| **Deduction / decomposition** | Decompose the discovery into sub-steps and reason structurally | MOOSE-Chem (`background + inspirations`), HypoRefine |

**Ungrounded baseline** is well-represented too: *zero-shot / few-shot "propose a novel idea" prompting*
(LLMs-are-Zero-Shot-Hypothesis-Proposers; the zero-shot arms of SciMON, MOOSE, HypoGeniC). Every grounded
method in this set is measured *against* such a prompt — so the hypothesis is directly testable with
existing baselines.

## 3. What the evidence says about the hypothesis (the through-line)

The papers provide repeated, quantitative support that **grounding helps**, with one important caveat:

- **SciMON**: zero-shot GPT-4 ideas are low-novelty/low-depth; adding inspiration retrieval (KG/semantic)
  and an *iterative novelty-boosting loop* (compare to k=20 nearest prior ideas, force "significantly
  different") raises human-judged novelty for **46–58%** of ideas and **never lowers it**.
- **MOOSE (open-domain, Yang et al.)**: novelty rises from **2.48 → 3.39** (expert-confirmed direction)
  as multi-step feedback grounding is added, *while validity stays ~3.9* — grounding raises novelty
  without sacrificing plausibility.
- **HypoGeniC (Zhou & Tan)**: inductive, data-grounded hypotheses beat few-shot prompting by
  **3.3–31.7%** accuracy and even beat fine-tuned RoBERTa/Llama-2 oracles trained on 1000 examples.
- **Literature Meets Data**: literature + data > few-shot by **8.97%**, > literature-alone by **15.75%**,
  > data-alone by **3.37%** (OOD); and grounded hypotheses improve *human* accuracy by up to **14.19%**.
- **HypoBench** (194 datasets, 12 domains): data-driven and literature+data methods dominate
  inference-only and zero-shot-generation baselines, especially OOD.
- **Evaluation side** — **RAG-Novelty** and **Literature-Grounded Novelty Assessment** (F1 0.79, κ 0.59,
  acc 0.81 vs. 0.68 zero-shot) both show grounding the *judgment* in retrieved papers beats ungrounded
  LLM-as-judge.

**The caveat (critical for experiment design).** *Novelty ≠ value.* Si et al. find AI ideas are judged
**more novel** (5.64 vs 4.84, p<0.01) but **slightly less feasible** than experts'; their follow-up
**Ideation–Execution Gap** shows that once ideas are actually executed, the LLM novelty advantage
*shrinks and rankings can flip*. SciMON shows grounding still leaves an **85%** gap to real paper ideas on
technical depth. **Implication:** the project should measure not just a novelty score but *grounded
usefulness* (downstream accuracy, ground-truth recovery, feasibility) — exactly what the "outcome
imagination / small experiments / literature comparison" grounding modes provide.

## 4. Common Methodologies

- **Retrieve-then-generate** (RAG over a paper corpus / KG): SciMON, ResearchAgent, Chain-of-Ideas, Nova,
  HypER, MOOSE-Chem.
- **Iterative refine-and-check loops**: compare to prior work and revise (SciMON novelty boosting), or
  update from data via a bandit reward (HypoGeniC), or agent–reviewer debate (ResearchAgent, Nova).
- **Decomposition**: `hypothesis = background + inspirations` (MOOSE-Chem); facet recombination
  (Scideator); literature∪data union (Literature Meets Data).
- **Closed-loop with experiments/simulation**: SGA bilevel optimization; downstream-accuracy as the
  empirical check (HypoGeniC/HypoBench/Literature Meets Data).
- **Controllable generation**: explicit novelty/feasibility dimensions (Learning-to-Generate-Ideas).

## 5. Standard Baselines (use these)

1. **Zero-shot prompting** — "propose a novel hypothesis for q." (ungrounded; the key control)
2. **Few-shot prompting** — a handful of demonstrations.
3. **Literature-only generation** — retrieve + generate, no data/experiment grounding.
4. **Data-only generation** — HypoGeniC (induction), no literature.
5. **Supervised oracle** (for data tasks) — fine-tuned RoBERTa / Llama as an upper-bound reference.
6. **Strong grounded systems** as toplines — SciMON, HypoRefine/Union, MOOSE-Chem.

## 6. Evaluation Metrics (use a mix; do not rely on a single novelty score)

- **Grounded utility**: downstream classification accuracy (IND + **OOD** for generalizability);
  human-decision-improvement; **Hypothesis/Feature Discovery Rate** and **Relationship Correctness**
  (HypoBench); rediscovery similarity to held-out ground-truth hypotheses (MOOSE-Chem).
- **Novelty / quality (human or LLM-judge)**: Novelty, Excitement, Feasibility, Expected-Effectiveness,
  Overall on 1–10 (Si et al.); Validness/Novelty/Helpfulness 1–5 (MOOSE); pairwise novelty (SchNovel).
- **Grounded novelty judging**: RAG-Novelty / Literature-Grounded assessment (retrieval-conditioned),
  reported with **Cohen's κ** for human agreement — preferred over bare LLM-as-judge.
- **Rigor**: blind review, multiple reviewers, Welch's t-test + Bonferroni, diversity/duplication rate
  (idea generators collapse to few unique ideas at scale — Si et al.).

## 7. Datasets in the Literature (and what we downloaded)

- **HypoGeniC / HypoBench tasks** (downloaded → `datasets/`): deceptive reviews, dreaddit (stress),
  AIGC detection (gptgc/llamagc), headline engagement, retweets, persuasive pairs, paper citations,
  journal; plus controllable **synthetic** tasks (election, admission, shoe, preference). HF-style JSON,
  shared across HypoGeniC, Literature Meets Data, and HypoBench → a single task vocabulary for ablations.
- **Hypothesis-agent literature** (downloaded): per-task PDFs + parsed papers for the literature-grounded
  conditions.
- **Idea-level benchmarks** (in cloned code / available): MOOSE-Chem (51 annotated 2024 chemistry papers),
  open-domain TOMATO (50 social-science papers), Si et al. ideation+review data, IdeaBench, ResearchBench,
  SchNovel (15k arXiv pairs).

## 8. Gaps and Opportunities (where this project can contribute)

1. **No head-to-head of grounding *mechanisms* under one protocol.** Each paper grounds *one* way and
   compares to ungrounded prompting; nobody has held task/model/metric fixed and ablated
   deduction vs. induction vs. proposal-writing vs. outcome-imagination vs. small-experiments vs.
   literature-comparison. **This is exactly the project's opening** — and the ChicagoHAI framework
   already implements the ungrounded baseline + induction + literature conditions on shared tasks.
2. **Novelty is over-trusted.** Given Si et al.'s ideation–execution flip, the project should report
   *grounded usefulness* alongside novelty, not novelty alone.
3. **Grounded *evaluation* of grounded *generation*.** Use RAG-Novelty / literature-grounded assessment
   to score ideas, avoiding the unreliable bare LLM-judge.
4. **Cost/benefit of grounding.** Outcome-imagination and small-experiments grounding cost compute;
   quantifying accuracy-per-token across mechanisms would be novel and practical.

## 9. Recommendations for Our Experiment

- **Backbone**: `code/hypogenic_chicagohai` (HypoGeniC + HypoRefine + Union). It already ships the
  *ungrounded baseline*, *induction*, and *literature-comparison* conditions on a shared task set with
  shared metrics — minimal glue to add the remaining grounding arms.
- **Datasets**: 3–4 HypoGeniC *real* tasks (deceptive_reviews, headline_binary, dreaddit/AIGC) + 2
  HypoBench *synthetic* controllable tasks (clean ground-truth recovery) + MOOSE-Chem for idea-level
  novelty. All already local in `datasets/` / `code/`.
- **Conditions (the ablation)**: (0) ungrounded zero/few-shot → (1) induction (HypoGeniC) →
  (2) literature-comparison (SciMON-style boosting / HypoRefine) → (3) literature+data Union →
  (4) outcome-imagination/small-experiment grounding (downstream-accuracy or SGA-style check) →
  (5) proposal-writing (full structured proposal + reviewer). Hold model and task fixed.
- **Metrics**: primary = downstream IND/OOD accuracy + HypoBench discovery rate / MOOSE-Chem rediscovery;
  secondary = grounded novelty (RAG-Novelty) + feasibility, with Cohen's κ for any human/LLM judging.
- **Models**: at least one strong API model + one open model (cross-model generalization, per HypoBench).
- **Statistics**: blind/randomized judging where human/LLM scores are used; Welch's t-test + multiple-
  comparison correction; report idea-diversity to catch mode collapse.

---

## 10. Per-Paper Notes

*(Structured notes extracted by a parallel deep-reading pass; the two ChicagoHAI/evaluation entries marked
"corrected" were re-read by the main agent after a reader confused* Literature Meets Data *with SciMON.)*

input.content:
# Resources Catalog — Scientific Idea Generation with Grounded Novelty

## Summary

All resources gathered for testing the hypothesis that **LLMs generate better scientific ideas when
novelty is grounded** (deduction, induction, proposal writing, outcome imagination, small experiments,
literature comparison) rather than via abstract/ungrounded prompts.

- **Papers downloaded**: 20 PDFs (`papers/`), curated from **218** unique candidates
  (`paper_search_results/all_papers_consolidated.json`).
- **Datasets**: 3 cloned repos (`datasets/`) covering the shared HypoGeniC / HypoBench task suite.
- **Code repositories**: 6 cloned (`code/`), incl. the ChicagoHAI framework that implements the core
  grounded vs. ungrounded conditions.
- Full analysis: `literature_review.md`. Per-directory detail: `papers/README.md`,
  `datasets/README.md`, `code/README.md`.

---

## Papers (20)

| # | Title | Year | arXiv | Grounding angle |
|---|-------|------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | Can LLMs Generate Novel Research Ideas? (100+ NLP researchers) | 2024 | 2409.04109 | Novelty **evaluation** (human study) |
| 2 | SciMON: Scientific Inspiration Machines Optimized for Novelty | 2023 | 2305.14259 | Literature comparison (novelty loop) |
| 3 | Hypothesis Generation with LLMs (HypoGeniC) | 2024 | 2404.04326 | Induction / data-driven |
| 4 | LLMs for Automated Open-domain Scientific Hypotheses Discovery (MOOSE) | 2023 | 2309.02726 | Induction + feedback |
| 5 | Literature Meets Data (HypoRefine / Union) | 2024 | 2410.17309 | Literature + induction + small-exp |
| 6 | HypoBench | 2025 | 2504.11524 | Benchmark (grounded) |
| 7 | Literature-Grounded Novelty Assessment of Scientific Ideas | 2025 | 2506.22026 | Grounded novelty **evaluation** |
| 8 | Evaluating & Enhancing LLMs for Novelty Assessment (SchNovel/RAG-Novelty) | 2024 | 2409.16605 | Grounded novelty **evaluation** |
| 9 | MOOSE-Chem (chemistry rediscovery) | 2024 | 2410.07076 | Deduction/decomposition + literature |
| 10 | LLM + Simulation as Bilevel Optimizers (SGA) | 2024 | 2405.09783 | Small experiments + outcome imagination |
| 11 | ResearchAgent | 2024 | 2404.07738 | Proposal writing (agentic) |
| 12 | Chain of Ideas | 2024 | 2410.13185 | Literature chain |
| 13 | Nova: Iterative Planning & Search for Novelty | 2024 | 2410.14255 | Planning/search for novelty |
| 14 | Scideator (facet recombination + novelty eval) | 2024 | 2409.14634 | Recombination + novelty eval |
| 15 | HypER: Literature-grounded Hypothesis Generation | 2025 | 2506.12937 | Literature-grounded + reasoning |
| 16 | LLMs are Zero-Shot Hypothesis Proposers | 2023 | 2311.05965 | **Ungrounded baseline** |
| 17 | Learning to Generate Research Idea with Dynamic Control | 2024 | 2412.14626 | Controlled novelty/feasibility |
| 18 | IdeaBench | 2024 | 2411.02429 | Benchmark (idea generation) |
| 19 | ResearchBench | 2025 | 2503.21248 | Benchmark (inspiration decomposition) |
| 20 | Survey on Hypothesis Generation in the Era of LLMs | 2025 | 2504.05496 | Survey |

See `papers/README.md` for abstracts and `literature_review.md` §10 for full structured notes.

## Datasets (3 repos, ~70 MB)

| Name | Source | Size | Task | Location | Notes |
|------|--------|------|------|----------|-------|
| HypoGeniC datasets | github.com/ChicagoHAI/HypoGeniC-datasets | ~29 MB | data-rule hypothesis gen → classification | `datasets/hypogenic_datasets/` | 9 real + 8 synthetic tasks; HF-JSON; 200 train each |
| HypoBench datasets | github.com/ChicagoHAI/HypoBench-datasets | ~32 MB | benchmark, controllable difficulty | `datasets/hypobench_datasets/` | same task vocab; ground-truth recovery |
| Hypothesis-agent datasets | github.com/ChicagoHAI/Hypothesis-agent-datasets | ~9 MB | literature for grounded methods | `datasets/hypothesis_agent_datasets/` | per-task PDFs + parsed papers |

Data files are git-ignored; re-clone via `datasets/README.md`. Idea-level benchmarks (MOOSE-Chem,
SchNovel, IdeaBench, ResearchBench) ship with the cloned code or are downloadable on demand.

## Code Repositories (6)

| Name | URL | Purpose | Location |
|------|-----|---------|----------|
| hypogenic_chicagohai ⭐ | github.com/ChicagoHAI/hypothesis-generation | **Primary backbone**: HypoGeniC (induction) + HypoRefine/Union (literature+data) + HypoBench; includes ungrounded baseline | `code/hypogenic_chicagohai/` |
| ai_researcher_si2024 ⭐ | github.com/NoviScl/AI-Researcher | Ideation agent + human-study ideas/reviews + Ideation–Execution Gap data | `code/ai_researcher_si2024/` |
| moose_chem | github.com/ZonglinY/MOOSE-Chem | Multi-agent chemistry hypothesis discovery + 51-paper annotated benchmark | `code/moose_chem/` |
| chain_of_ideas | github.com/DAMO-NLP-SG/CoI-Agent | Literature-chain ideation + Idea-Arena eval | `code/chain_of_ideas/` |
| scimon_clbd | github.com/eaglew/clbd | SciMON: inspiration retrieval + iterative novelty-boosting loop | `code/scimon_clbd/` |

(`code/README.md` has install notes, entry points, and the mechanism→repo map.) Bulky data/model
artifacts are git-ignored via `code/.gitignore`.

## Resource Gathering Notes

### Search strategy
Used the local **paper-finder** service (diligent + fast modes) with 4 complementary queries
(grounding-novelty, hypothesis-generation, idea-generation, novelty-assessment), yielding 218 unique
papers after dedup. Ranked by relevance (129 at 3/3) and curated 20 spanning every grounding mechanism +
the ungrounded baseline + evaluation. arXiv IDs resolved via Semantic Scholar (with arXiv title-search
fallback when rate-limited); PDFs pulled from arXiv (20/20 succeeded). Repos discovered via the papers'
code links and the UChicago/ChicagoHAI authorship of the core data-driven line.

### Selection criteria
Coverage of each named grounding mode; the seminal evaluation study (Si et al.); shared-task benchmarks
enabling a clean ablation; code availability; recency (2023–2025) plus the highest-cited foundations.

### Challenges & workarounds
- Semantic Scholar 429 rate-limits → arXiv title-search fallback (all 20 resolved).
- A `head`-truncated pipe initially broke a JSONL save → re-ran with full output capture.
- **Verification catch**: the parallel deep-reader confused *Literature Meets Data* with *SciMON*; the
  main agent re-read both that paper and the SchNovel paper directly and corrected the notes.

### Quality/verification
20/20 PDFs verified as valid (`%PDF` header + size). Core-paper notes spot-checked against the PDFs;
one confusion found and fixed (above). Dataset schemas inspected (HF-JSON, sample records confirmed).

## Recommendations for Experiment Design

1. **Primary dataset(s)**: HypoGeniC real tasks (deceptive_reviews, headline_binary, dreaddit/AIGC) +
   2 HypoBench synthetic controllable tasks + MOOSE-Chem for idea-level novelty.
2. **Baselines / conditions**: ungrounded zero/few-shot → induction (HypoGeniC) → literature-comparison
   (SciMON-style / HypoRefine) → literature+data Union → outcome/small-experiment grounding →
   proposal-writing; model & task held fixed.
3. **Metrics**: downstream IND/OOD accuracy + HypoBench discovery rate / MOOSE-Chem rediscovery (primary);
   grounded novelty (RAG-Novelty) + feasibility, Cohen's κ for judging (secondary). Report idea diversity.
4. **Code to reuse**: `hypogenic_chicagohai` as backbone; SciMON novelty loop; MOOSE-Chem benchmark;
   `ai_researcher_si2024` review data/agent for human-aligned evaluation.

| Name | Source | Size | Task | Location | Notes |
|------|--------|------|------|----------|-------|
| HypoGeniC datasets | github.com/ChicagoHAI/HypoGeniC-datasets | ~29 MB | data-rule hypothesis gen → classification | `datasets/hypogenic_datasets/` | 9 real + 8 synthetic tasks; HF-JSON; 200 train each |
| HypoBench datasets | github.com/ChicagoHAI/HypoBench-datasets | ~32 MB | benchmark, controllable difficulty | `datasets/hypobench_datasets/` | same task vocab; ground-truth recovery |
| Hypothesis-agent datasets | github.com/ChicagoHAI/Hypothesis-agent-datasets | ~9 MB | literature for grounded methods | `datasets/hypothesis_agent_datasets/` | per-task PDFs + parsed papers |

Data files are git-ignored; re-clone via `datasets/README.md`. Idea-level benchmarks (MOOSE-Chem,
SchNovel, IdeaBench, ResearchBench) ship with the cloned code or are downloadable on demand.

## Code Repositories (6)

| Name | URL | Purpose | Location |
|------|-----|---------|----------|
| hypogenic_chicagohai ⭐ | github.com/ChicagoHAI/hypothesis-generation | **Primary backbone**: HypoGeniC (induction) + HypoRefine/Union (literature+data) + HypoBench; includes ungrounded baseline | `code/hypogenic_chicagohai/` |
| ai_researcher_si2024 ⭐ | github.com/NoviScl/AI-Researcher | Ideation agent + human-study ideas/reviews + Ideation–Execution Gap data | `code/ai_researcher_si2024/` |
| moose_chem | github.com/ZonglinY/MOOSE-Chem | Multi-agent chemistry hypothesis discovery + 51-paper annotated benchmark | `code/moose_chem/` |
| chain_of_ideas | github.com/DAMO-NLP-SG/CoI-Agent | Literature-chain ideation + Idea-Arena eval | `code/chain_of_ideas/` |
| scimon_clbd | github.com/eaglew/clbd | SciMON: inspiration retrieval + iterative novelty-boosting loop | `code/scimon_clbd/` |

(`code/README.md` has install notes, entry points, and the mechanism→repo map.) Bulky data/model
artifacts are git-ignored via `code/.gitignore`.

## Resource Gathering Notes

### Search strategy
Used the local **paper-finder** service (diligent + fast modes) with 4 complementary queries
(grounding-novelty, hypothesis-generation, idea-generation, novelty-assessment), yielding 218 unique
papers after dedup. Ranked by relevance (129 at 3/3) and curated 20 spanning every grounding mechanism +
the ungrounded baseline + evaluation. arXiv IDs resolved via Semantic Scholar (with arXiv title-search
fallback when rate-limited); PDFs pulled from arXiv (20/20 succeeded). Repos discovered via the papers'
code links and the UChicago/ChicagoHAI authorship of the core data-driven line.

### Selection criteria
Coverage of each named grounding mode; the seminal evaluation study (Si et al.); shared-task benchmarks
enabling a clean ablation; code availability; recency (2023–2025) plus the highest-cited foundations.

### Challenges & workarounds
- Semantic Scholar 429 rate-limits → arXiv title-search fallback (all 20 resolved).
- A `head`-truncated pipe initially broke a JSONL save → re-ran with full output capture.
- **Verification catch**: the parallel deep-reader confused *Literature Meets Data* with *SciMON*; the
  main agent re-read both that paper and the SchNovel paper directly and corrected the notes.

### Quality/verification
20/20 PDFs verified as valid (`%PDF` header + size). Core-paper notes spot-checked against the PDFs;
one confusion found and fixed (above). Dataset schemas inspected (HF-JSON, sample records confirmed).

## Recommendations for Experiment Design

1. **Primary dataset(s)**: HypoGeniC real tasks (deceptive_reviews, headline_binary, dreaddit/AIGC) +
   2 HypoBench synthetic controllable tasks + MOOSE-Chem for idea-level novelty.
2. **Baselines / conditions**: ungrounded zero/few-shot → induction (HypoGeniC) → literature-comparison
   (SciMON-style / HypoRefine) → literature+data Union → outcome/small-experiment grounding →
   proposal-writing; model & task held fixed.
3. **Metrics**: downstream IND/OOD accuracy + HypoBench discovery rate / MOOSE-Chem rediscovery (primary);
   grounded novelty (RAG-Novelty) + feasibility, Cohen's κ for judging (secondary). Report idea diversity.
4. **Code to reuse**: `hypogenic_chicagohai` as backbone; SciMON novelty loop; MOOSE-Chem benchmark;
   `ai_researcher_si2024` review data/agent for human-aligned evaluation.

chk(){ test -e "$1" && echo "✓ $1 ($(du -sh "$1" 2>/dev/null|cut -f1))" || echo "✗ MISSING $1"; }
chk papers; chk papers/README.md
chk datasets; chk datasets/README.md; chk datasets/.gitignore
chk code; chk code/README.md; chk code/.gitignore

echo

echo
echo "=== all 20 PDFs valid? (header check) ==="
bad=0; for f in papers/*.pdf; do [ "$(head -c4 "$f")" = "%PDF" ] || { echo "BAD $f"; bad=1; }; done; [ $bad -eq 0 ] && echo "✓ all 20 PDFs valid"
echo
echo "=== bulky data git-ignored? ==="
git ls-files --others --exclude-standard -z 2>/dev/null | du -ch --files0-from=- 2>/dev/null | tail -1 | sed 's/^/git would stage: /'

content.text:
All deliverables are complete and verified. Here's a summary of the resource-gathering phase.

## ✅ Resource Finding Complete — *Scientific Idea Generation with Grounded Novelty*

**Environment**: Isolated `uv` venv + `pyproject.toml` (pypdf, requests, arxiv, httpx) — no contamination of any shared environment.

### What was gathered

**📄 Papers — 20 PDFs** (curated from **218** unique candidates via paper-finder's diligent mode across 4 complementary queries; full list in `paper_search_results/all_papers_consolidated.json`). The set deliberately spans every grounding mechanism in the hypothesis plus the ungrounded baseline and the evaluation literature:
- *Literature comparison*: SciMON, Chain-of-Ideas, Nova, RAG-Novelty
- *Induction/data-driven*: HypoGeniC, open-domain MOOSE, HypoBench
- *Literature + data*: Literature Meets Data (HypoRefine/Union)
- *Small experiments / outcome imagination*: SGA bilevel optimizers
- *Deduction/decomposition*: MOOSE-Chem
- *Proposal writing*: ResearchAgent, Si et al. agent
- *Ungrounded baseline*: Zero-Shot Hypothesis Proposers
- *Evaluation*: Si et al. 100-researcher study, SchNovel, Literature-Grounded Novelty Assessment, IdeaBench, ResearchBench

**📊 Datasets — 3 repos** (`datasets/`, git-ignored): HypoGeniC, HypoBench, and Hypothesis-agent datasets — a **shared task suite** (deceptive reviews, headlines, retweets, stress, AIGC + controllable synthetic tasks) usable across all grounding conditions.

**💻 Code — 5 repos** (`code/`, bulky data git-ignored): `hypogenic_chicagohai` ⭐ (the recommended backbone — it already implements *ungrounded baseline + induction + literature-comparison* on shared tasks/metrics), `ai_researcher_si2024`, `moose_chem`, `chain_of_ideas`, `scimon_clbd`.

### Key synthesis findings (`literature_review.md`)
- **Grounding consistently helps**: SciMON's novelty loop raises novelty for 46–58% of ideas (never lowers); HypoGeniC beats few-shot by 3.3–31.7%; Literature+Data beats few-shot by 8.97% and improves *human* accuracy by up to 14.19%.
- **Critical caveat for experiment design**: novelty ≠ value. Si et al. find AI ideas judged *more novel but less feasible*, and their Ideation–Execution Gap shows the advantage erodes after execution → measure *grounded usefulness* (downstream accuracy, ground-truth recovery), not novelty alone.
- **Gap this project fills**: nobody has held task/model/metric fixed and ablated the six grounding mechanisms head-to-head — and the ChicagoHAI framework makes that ablation cheap.

### Quality note
During the parallel deep-read, one reader confused *Literature Meets Data* with *SciMON*; I caught it by spot-checking against the PDF and **re-read both that paper and the SchNovel paper directly** to correct the notes (flagged in the docs).

**Deliverables**: `papers/`, `datasets/`, `code/` (each with README + gitignore), `literature_review.md` (10 sections + 20 structured per-paper notes), `resources.md`, and `.resource_finder_complete`. Bulky data (526 MB) is correctly excluded from git — only ~60 MB (the PDFs + source + docs) would be committed. The experiment-runner phase can begin.
